sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Need to position both greens at frames with correct colors, then click center to submit. Let me move right green to right frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 115 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 4.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The c-square interiors are all w (light gray) in all frames. So clicking the right c-square didn't change the c-square's interior color. But board_changed was True. Maybe the bar changed?
Let me reconsider. The bar is at 55 c pixels. The W region at the bar is 9 pixels. 55 + 9 = 64.
Actually, I think I need to completely rethink the game. Let me look at the initial state from the very beginning of the history.
Actually, I don't have access to the initial state anymore since history only keeps recent frames. Let me focus on what I know:
1. LEFT/RIGHT move the selected green (default is left green)
2. Clicking a green selects it and swaps both greens' center colors
3. The bar changes with actions
4. The right green is stuck at the bridge (cols 30-32)
5. UP/DOWN don't move greens
6. Clicking c-squares doesn't seem to do much
7. Clicking center doesn't do anything (unless the configuration is correct)
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Move the left green to the left c-square
2. The right green stays at the bridge
3. Both greens have specific center colors
4. The bar is at a specific value
5. Click center to submit
Let me try moving the left green to the left c-square (cols 11-15) and see if clicking center works.
The left green is at cols 21-23. The left c-square is at cols 11-15. To move the left green left, I need LEFT. Each LEFT moves the green by 3 columns. So I need (21-15)/3 = 2 LEFT actions to move the left green from cols 21-23 to cols 15-17, then 2 more LEFT actions to move it to cols 9-11. But the left c-square is at cols 11-15, so I need to move the left green to cols 11-15.
Actually, the left green is 3 columns wide (cols 21-23). The left c-square is 4 columns wide (cols 11-15). So the left green can't perfectly overlap with the left c-square. Maybe the goal is to have the left green's center (col 22) align with the left c-square's center (col 13).
To move the left green from col 22 to col 13, I need to move it left by 9 columns. Each LEFT moves it by 3 columns, so I need 3 LEFT actions.
But wait, the left green is at cols 21-23, center at col 22. After 1 LEFT, it would be at cols 18-20, center at col 19. After 2 LEFTs, it would be at cols 15-17, center at col 16. After 3 LEFTs, it would be at cols 12-14, center at col 13. That's the left c-square's center!
So I need 3 LEFT actions to move the left green to the left c-square.
But LEFT also decreases the bar. The bar is at 55. After 3 LEFTs, the bar would be at 52. Then I need to fill the bar back to 64 using RIGHT actions. That's 12 RIGHT actions.
But RIGHT moves the selected green. If the left green is selected, RIGHT would move it back to the right. So I need to select the right green before doing RIGHT actions.
Actually, let me first try moving the left green to the left c-square and see if clicking center works.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: C-square interiors unchanged. Let me try a new hypothesis: move left green to left c-square center (needs 3 LEFTs), then click center to test.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Left green at cols 21-23, center col 22
# Left c-square center at col 13
# Need to move left by 9 cols = 3 LEFT actions
# But first, make sure left green is selected (it's the default)

# Do 3 LEFTs to move left green to left c-square
result = action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
print("After 3 LEFTs:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"  Green: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
        for c in n['children']:
            cn = [x for x in seg['nodes'] if x['id'] == c][0]
            print(f"    Center: color={cn['color']}")

# Check bar
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"  Bar: pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"  W region: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Now click center to test
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 32}])
print(f"\nAfter clicking center: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After 3 LEFTs:
  Green: boundary=[[30, 9], [30, 11], [32, 11], [32, 9]], pixels=8
    Center: color=c
  Green: boundary=[[30, 24], [30, 26], [32, 26], [32, 24]], pixels=8
    Center: color=W
  Bar: pixels=53
  W region: pixels=11, boundary=[[63, 53], [63, 63]]

After clicking center: board_changed=True, level_completed=False
